name: Maryland Beaten Biscuits
recipeInstructions:
(The oldest and most famous biscuit recipe of the Chesapeake Bay region originated on the plantations of southern Maryland. Its execution is best described by Joanne Pritchett, whose great‑great grandmother was a cook on a St. Mary's plantation: "Honey, every time I know I'm going to make these biscuits, I get myself good and mad... It's very simple. First sift the flour and salt together in a bowl. Some people like to use Crisco or something like that, but I believe in lard—it gives it that certain taste.
Cut the lard into the flour with the tips of your fingers, working quickly until everything is well mixed. Then add cold water a little at a time, stirring until you have a stiff dough. Turn the dough out onto a floured surface; if your table is weak, honey, the legs will fall right off!
Using an axe or a large mallet, flatten the dough into a ball (about the size of a small egg) and pound it flat with a few pokes from a fork. Beat the dough until it blisters slightly—this should take about 20‑30 minutes—and then shape it into biscuits.
Bake on a hot 425 °F oven for about 20–25 minutes, or until golden brown. Serve immediately and enjoy! Makes about 3 dozen biscuits.)
